lessons.title: Hello, Outline
lessons.desc: Outline infers every type automatically — no annotations, no declarations. Run this and watch the Inference panel fill in the types for you.

lessons.title: Variables: let & var
lessons.desc: `let` declares an immutable binding; `var` declares a mutable one. GCP enforces mutability at the type level — assigning to a `let` binding is a compile-time error.
lessons.code:
let x = 42;           // immutable — cannot be reassigned
var y = 10;           // mutable   — can be reassigned

y = y + 1;            // ✓  var is mutable
y = y * 2;            // ✓

// x = 99;            // ✗ [semantic] not assignable  ← compile-time error

let result = x + y;   // 42 + 22 = 64
result;

lessons.title: Built-in Functions
lessons.desc: Outline ships with a small set of pre-defined global functions. They are registered in the type system so the inferencer recognises them — no imports needed.

lessons.title: Lambda Expressions
lessons.desc: Functions are first-class values written as `param -> body`. The parameter and return types are inferred from usage — no annotations needed.

lessons.title: Currying — Every Function is Curried
lessons.desc: All Outline functions take exactly one argument. Multi-argument functions are written as nested lambdas. GCP infers the full `A → B → C` chain and supports partial application automatically.

lessons.title: Higher-Order Functions
lessons.desc: Functions that accept or return other functions. GCP tracks the complete type signature through every HOF layer — no annotations, no type parameters.

lessons.title: Recursive Functions
lessons.desc: GCP handles self-referential `let` bindings. Recursive functions are inferred as easily as non-recursive ones.

lessons.title: Defining Entities
lessons.desc: Entity types are named product types (records / structs). Fields are typed and accessed with dot notation. GCP infers the exact type of every field access.

lessons.title: Anonymous Entities
lessons.desc: You don't need to declare an outline to use entity types. Anonymous entity literals are inferred structurally — GCP synthesises the type from the fields present.

lessons.title: Entity Inheritance
lessons.desc: An outline can extend another outline, inheriting all its fields. Subtype instances can be used wherever the base type is expected — structural subtyping.

lessons.title: Copy Constructor: `this{...}` Pattern
lessons.desc: The `this{field=value}` expression creates a shallow copy of the current entity with some fields overridden. This is Outline's idiomatic immutable-update pattern — the original is unchanged, the return type is the SAME concrete type as the receiver.

lessons.title: with...as Expression
lessons.desc: The `with expr as name { ... }` expression evaluates a value, binds it to a name for the duration of a block, and automatically calls open/close lifecycle hooks if present. Great for scoped resources.

lessons.title: Mutable Entity Fields
lessons.desc: `var` fields in entities can be mutated in place. `let` fields are immutable — GCP catches assignments to them at compile time.

lessons.title: Sum Types
lessons.desc: Sum types (also called union/variant types) describe a value that is exactly ONE of several variants. Outline uses `outline Name = A | B | C` syntax.

lessons.title: Variant Entities (ADT)
lessons.desc: Sum type variants can carry data — like algebraic data types in Haskell. Each variant is a mini-entity. Pattern matching unpacks the fields inline.

lessons.title: Open Poly: Implicit Union from Branches
lessons.desc: When an `if/else` or `match` returns different types, GCP automatically creates a Poly (union) type for the result. No annotation needed — the union is inferred.

lessons.title: Poly in Function Signatures
lessons.desc: A Poly return type propagates through the call chain — GCP tracks every branch and unifies them. This is the foundation of Outline's dynamic type flow.

lessons.title: Arrays & Ranges
lessons.desc: Arrays are first-class values. A range `[a...b]` creates an array from a to b. All operations (map, filter, reduce) are inferred end-to-end.

lessons.title: Data Pipeline
lessons.desc: Chain filter, map, and reduce to build readable data pipelines. The inferred type flows through every step without annotation.

lessons.title: some / every — Short-Circuit Predicates
lessons.desc: `.some` stops scanning on the first TRUE. `.every` stops on the first FALSE. Both short-circuit — cleaner than a loop with a flag variable.

lessons.title: take_while / drop_while — Prefix Splitting
lessons.desc: Split an array at the first element that breaks a condition. Unlike filter, both respect position — once the condition fails, scanning stops.

lessons.title: Structural Type Synthesis
lessons.desc: GCP synthesises the full entity type from HOW functions USE it — not from any declaration. TypeScript requires explicit annotations; Haskell requires named type declarations. Outline needs nothing.

lessons.title: Backward Constraint Propagation
lessons.desc: Outline's inference runs in REVERSE: the type of an entity is determined by how a function USES it, not where it comes from. This lets GCP catch field-missing errors through multi-level HOF chains that no other language type-checks without annotations.

lessons.title: Future this: Self-Type Extension Chaining
lessons.desc: Methods that return `this` always return the CONCRETE receiver — not just the declared type. So when a sub-entity extends a base entity and calls a base method that returns `this`, the result is the sub-entity. This makes base→sub method chains work transparently with zero type annotations.

lessons.title: Generic Entities with Type Parameters
lessons.desc: Outline entities support generic type parameters. The `Base<i,o>` pattern allows a single entity definition to work over any type. Combined with `this{...}`, you get a fully generic, inheritable container.

lessons.title: Literal Types as Constant Fields
lessons.desc: `#"value"` declares a literal type — the type IS the value. The field is auto-filled at construction and can never be overridden. Default-value fields auto-fill but can be overridden.

lessons.title: Full Dynamic Inference: Church Numerals
lessons.desc: GCP can infer types through arbitrarily complex chains — including Church numerals (numbers encoded purely as higher-order functions). This demonstrates the full power of constraint-based inference with zero annotations anywhere.

lessons.title: console API
lessons.desc: The `console` module provides three logging levels. Output appears in the Console tab with colour-coded icons so you can distinguish info, warnings and errors at a glance.

lessons.title: math Library
lessons.desc: The `math` module exposes common numeric functions and constants. All argument and return types are inferred — no imports or type casts needed.

lessons.title: date Library
lessons.desc: The `date` module gives you the current date/time and parses date strings. Date fields (year, month, day, hour, minute, second) are all inferred as Int.

lessons.title: string — String Manipulation
lessons.desc: The `string` module provides everything you need for string processing: splitting, joining, case conversion, searching, slicing, padding and repeating — all curried and fully type-inferred.

lessons.title: JSON — Serialize & Deserialize
lessons.desc: The `JSON` module converts between Outline values and JSON strings. `stringify` handles all value types (entities, arrays, primitives). `parse` returns a fully typed Outline value tree.

lessons.title: Http — HTTP Requests
lessons.desc: The `Http` module makes real HTTP requests. `get(url)` returns `{ status, body, ok, json }`. Use `.json()` to parse the response body as Outline data. Requests time out after 15 seconds.

lessons.title: Http — POST Requests
lessons.desc: Use `Http.post(url)(body)` to send plain-text bodies and `Http.post_json(url)(body)` to send JSON. The JSON body can be an Outline entity — it is serialized automatically.

lessons.title: IO — Sandbox File System
lessons.desc: The `IO` module provides an in-memory sandbox file system — scoped to the current execution. Write, append, read, check existence, delete, and list files. Great for scripting patterns without touching the real disk.
